items.q: Why is HDI a better measure of development than GNI per capita?
items.correct: HDI combines income, life expectancy and education, capturing quality of life — GNI per capita is just average income and hides inequality and non-economic wellbeing.
items.misconception: Because HDI measures how happy people are.
items.random_wrong: Because HDI is easier to calculate than GNI.
items.why: Students treat HDI as a happiness index. It is broader than GNI but still only captures three measurable dimensions — not subjective wellbeing or environmental quality.

items.q: How are fold mountains like the Himalayas formed?
items.correct: By the collision of two continental plates (India and Eurasia) where neither can subduct, so the crust crumples and is forced upwards over millions of years.
items.misconception: Fold mountains form when one plate slides under another into the mantle.
items.random_wrong: Fold mountains form from giant volcanoes erupting upwards.
items.why: Students confuse continent-continent collision with subduction. Continental crust is too buoyant to subduct, so it crumples — distinct from oceanic-continental boundaries like the Andes.

items.q: What does Frank's dependency theory argue?
items.correct: That LICs (periphery) are kept poor by exploitative trade relationships with HICs (core) — underdevelopment is actively produced by capitalism, not a stage that countries grow out of.
items.misconception: Dependency theory says LICs are poor because they depend too much on foreign aid.
items.random_wrong: Dependency theory is the same as Rostow's modernisation theory.
items.why: Dependency theory is structural and critical, opposing Rostow. The 'dependency' is on the global economic system, not aid. Students confuse the word 'dependency' with aid reliance, missing the political-economic critique.

items.q: Whose 1966 model explains migration through push, pull and obstacles?
items.why: It's Everett Lee. “Walt Rostow” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What term describes money sent home by migrants?
items.why: It's Remittances. “FDI” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately how many international migrants worldwide (in millions)?
items.why: Exact answer: 280. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
